New York State Testing Program
Grade 3 English Language Arts
Released Questions from 2025 Exams
Background
As in past years, the New York State Education Department (NYSED) is releasing large portions of the 2025 NYS Grades 
3–8 English Language Arts and Mathematics test materials for review, discussion, and use.
For 2025, at least 75 percent of the test questions that appeared on the 2025 tests and counted toward students' 
scores are included in these released materials. This includes all constructed-response questions. Additionally, NYSED
is providing information about the released passages; the associated text complexity for each passage; and a map 
that details what learning standards each released question measures and the correct response to each question. 
These released materials will help students, families, educators, and the public better understand the tests and 
NYSED’s expectations forstudents.
Understanding English Language Arts Questions
Multiple-Choice Questions
Multiple-choice questions are designed to assess the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards in 
English Language Arts (ELA). These questions ask students to analyze different aspects of a given text, including 
central idea, style elements, character and plot development, and vocabulary. Almost all questions, including 
vocabulary questions, will be answered correctly only if the student comprehends and makes use of the whole 
passage.
For multiple-choice questions, students select the correct response from four answer choices. Multiple-choice 
questions assess reading standards in a variety of ways. Some ask students to analyze aspects of text or vocabulary. 
Many questions require students to combine skills. For example, questions may ask students to identify a segment of 
text that best supports the central idea. To answer these questions correctly, a student must first comprehend the 
central idea and then show understanding of how that idea is supported. Questions tend to require more than rote 
recall or identification.
Two-Credit Constructed-Response Questions
Two-credit constructed-response questions are designed to assess New York State P–12 Reading and Language 
Standards. These are single questions in which a student uses textual evidence to support their answer to an 
inferential question. These questions ask the student to make an inference (a claim, position, or conclusion) based on 
their analysis of the passage, and then provide two pieces of text-based evidence to support their answer.
The purpose of the two-credit constructed-response questions is to assess a student’s ability to comprehend and 
analyze text. In responding to these questions, students are expected to write in complete sentences. Responses 
require no more than three complete sentences. The rubric used for evaluating two-credit constructed-response 
questions can be found in the grade-level Educator Guides at https://www.nysed.gov/state-assessment/ grades-3-8-
ela-math-and-science-test-manuals.
THE STATE EDUCATION DEPARTMENT / THE UNIVERSITY OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK / ALBANY, NY 12234
New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards Alignment
The alignment to the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards for ELA is intended to identify the 
analytic skills necessary to successfully answer each question. However, some questions measure proficiencies 
described in multiple standards, including writing and additional reading and language standards. For example, twocredit and four-credit constructed-response questions require students to first conduct the analyses described in the 
mapped standard and then produce written responses that are rated based on writing standards. To gain greater 
insight into the measurement focus for constructed-response questions, please refer to the rubrics.
These Released Questions Do Not Comprise a “Mini Test”
To ensure it is possible to develop future tests, some content must remain secure. This document is not intended to 
be representative of the entire test, to show how operational tests look, or to provide information about how 
teachers should administer the test; rather, its purpose is to provide an overview of how the test reflects the demands 
of the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards.
The released questions do not represent the full spectrum of the standards assessed on the State tests, nor do they 
represent the full spectrum of how the standards should be taught and assessed in the classroom. It should not be 
assumed that a particular standard will be measured by an identical question in future assessments.
2025 Grade 3 ELA Test Text Complexity Metrics for
Released Questions 
Selecting high-quality, grade-appropriate passages requires both objective text 
complexity metrics and expert judgment. For the Grades 3–8 assessments based on the 
New York State P-12 Next Generation Learning Standards for English Language Arts, both 
quantitative and qualitative rubrics are used to determine the complexity of the texts and 
their appropriate placement within a grade-level ELA exam.
Quantitative measures of text complexity are used to measure aspects of text complexity 
that are difficult for a human reader to evaluate when examining a text. These aspects 
include word frequency, word length, sentence length, and text cohesion. These aspects 
are efficiently measured by computer programs. While quantitative text complexity 
metrics are a helpful start, they are not definitive.
Qualitative measures are a crucial complement to quantitative measures. Using 
qualitative measures of text complexity involves making an informed decision about the 
difficulty of a text in terms of one or more factors discernible to a human reader applying 
trained judgment to the task. To qualitatively determine the complexity of a text, NYS 
educators use a rubric composed of five factors; four of these factors are required and 
one factor is optional. The required criteria are: meaning, text structure, language 
features, and knowledge demands. The optional factor, graphics, is used only if a graphic 
appears in the text.
To make the final determination as to whether a text is at grade level and thus 
appropriate to be included on a Grades 3–8 assessment, New York State uses a two-step 
review process, which is an industry best practice. First, all prospective passages undergo 
quantitative text complexity analysis using three text complexity measures. If at least two 
of the three measures suggest that the passage is grade-appropriate, the passage then 
moves to the second step, which is the qualitative review using the text-complexity 
rubrics. Only passages that are determined appropriate by at least two of three 
quantitative measures of complexity and are determined appropriate by the qualitative 
measure of complexity are deemed appropriate for use on the exam.

TIPS FOR TAKING THE TEST
Here are some ideas to help you do your best:
• Read the whole passage before you answer the questions. Most questions will only make 
sense after you read the whole passage.
• You might need to read the passage more than once to answer a question. 
• Read each question carefully. Take your time.
• A question may include a quote from a passage. You might need to review both the
quote and the whole passage to answer the question.
When you write your answers
• make sure to answer the whole question; 
• use examples or details from the text; 
• write in complete sentences; and
• use correct spelling, grammar, capitalization, and punctuation.

Background
As in past years, SED is releasing large portions of the 2025 NYS Grades 3–8 English Language Arts and 
Mathematics test materials for review, discussion, and use. 
For 2025, included in these released materials are at least 75 percent of the test questions that appeared on 
the 2025 tests (including all constructed-response questions) that counted toward students’ scores. 
Additionally, SED is also providing a map that details what each released question measures and the correct 
response to each question. These released materials will help students, families, educators, and the public 
better understand the tests and the New York State Education Department’s expectations for students. 
Understanding Math Questions
Multiple-Choice Questions 
Multiple-choice questions are designed to assess the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning 
Standards for Mathematics. Mathematics multiple-choice questions will be used mainly to assess standard 
algorithms and conceptual standards. Multiple-choice questions incorporate both the grade-level standards 
and the “Standards for Mathematical Practices.” Many questions are framed within the context of real-world 
applications or require students to complete multiple steps. Likewise, many of these questions are linked to 
more than one standard, drawing on the simultaneous application of multiple skills and concepts. 
One-Credit Constructed-Response Questions 
One-credit constructed-response questions require students to complete a task and provide only their final 
answer. These one-credit questions will often require multiple steps, assessing procedural skills, as well as 
conceptual understanding and application. While students may show how they arrived at their final answer, 
only the final answer will be scored. 
Two-Credit Constructed-Response Questions 
Two-credit constructed-response questions require students to complete tasks and show their work. These 
two-credit response questions will often require multiple steps, the application of multiple mathematics 
skills, and real-world applications. Many of the short-response questions will cover conceptual and 
application standards. 
Three-Credit Constructed-Response Questions 
Three-credit constructed-response questions ask students to show their work in completing two or more 
tasks or a more extensive problem. These three-credit response questions allow students to show their 
understanding of mathematical procedures, conceptual understanding, and application. Three-credit 
response questions may also assess student reasoning and the ability to critique the arguments of others. The 
scoring rubric for all constructed-response questions can be found in the grade-level Educator Guides at 
https://www.nysed.gov/state-assessment/grades-3-8-ela-and-math-test-manuals.
New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards Alignment 
The alignment(s) to the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards for Mathematics is/are 
intended to identify the primary analytic skills necessary to successfully answer each question. However, 
some questions measure proficiencies described in multiple standards, including a balanced combination of 
procedure and conceptual understanding. For example, two-credit and three-credit constructed-response 
questions require students to show an understanding of mathematical procedures, concepts, and 
applications. 
These Released Questions Do Not Comprise a “Mini Test” 
To ensure it is possible to develop future tests, some content must remain secure. This document is not 
intended to be representative of the entire test, to show how operational tests look, or to provide 
information about how teachers should administer the test; rather, its purpose is to provide an overview of 
how the test reflects the demands of the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards. 
The released questions do not represent the full spectrum of the standards assessed on the State tests, nor 
do they represent the full spectrum of how the standards should be taught and assessed in the classroom. It 
should not be assumed that a particular standard will be measured by an identical question in future 
assessments.

Background
As in past years, SED is releasing large portions of the 2024 NYS Grades 3–8 English Language Arts and Mathematics
test materialsfor review, discussion, and use.
For 2024, included in these released materials are at least 75 percent of the test questions that appeared on the
2024 tests (including all constructed-response questions) that counted toward students’ scores. Additionally, SED is
providing information about the released passages; the associated text complexity for each passage; and a map that
details what learning standards each released question measures and the correct response to each question. These
released materials will help students, families, educators, and the public better understand the tests and the New
York State Education Department’s expectations for students.
Understanding ELA Questions
Multiple-ChoiceQuestions
Multiple-choice questions are designed to assess the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards in
English Language Arts. These questions ask students to analyze different aspects of a given text, including central
idea, style elements, character and plot development, and vocabulary. Almost all questions, including vocabulary
questions, will be answered correctly only if the student comprehends and makes use of the whole passage.
For multiple-choice questions, students select the correct response from four answer choices. Multiple-choice
questions assess reading standards in a variety of ways. Some ask students to analyze aspects of text or vocabulary.
Many questions require students to combine skills. For example, questions may ask students to identify a segment of
text that best supports the central idea. To answer these questions correctly, a student must first comprehend the
central idea and then show understanding of how that idea is supported. Questions tend to require more than rote
recall or identification.
Two-Credit Constructed-ResponseQuestions
Two-credit constructed-response questions are designed to assess New York State P–12 Reading and Language
Standards. These are single questions in which a student uses textual evidence to support his or her answer to an
inferential question. These questions ask the student to make an inference (a claim, position, or conclusion) based on
their analysis of the passage, and then provide two pieces of text-based evidence to support their answer.
The purpose of the two-credit constructed-response questions is to assess a student’s ability to comprehend and
analyze text. In responding to these questions, students are expected to write in complete sentences. Responses
require no more than three complete sentences. The rubric used for evaluating two-credit constructed-response 
questions can be found in the grade-level Educator Guides at https://www.nysed.gov/state-assessment/
grades-3-8-ela-math-and-science-test-manuals.
New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards Alignment
The alignment to the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards for English Language Arts is intended
to identify the analytic skills necessary to successfully answer each question. However, some questions measure
proficiencies described in multiple standards, including writing and additional reading and language standards. For
example, two-credit and four-credit constructed-response questions require students to first conduct the analyses
described in the mapped standard and then produce written responses that are rated based on writing
standards. To gain greater insight into the measurement focus for constructed-response questions, please refer to
the rubrics.
These Released Questions Do Not Comprise a “Mini Test”
To ensure it is possible to develop future tests, some content must remain secure. This document is not intended
to be representative of the entire test, to show how operational tests look, or to provide information about how
teachers should administer the test; rather, its purpose is to provide an overview of how the test reflects the demands
of the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards.
The released questions do not represent the full spectrum of the standards assessed on the State tests, nor do they
represent the full spectrum of how the standards should be taught and assessed in the classroom. It should not be
assumed that a particular standard will be measured by an identical question in future assessments.
2024 Grade 3 ELA Test Text Complexity Metrics for
Released Questions 
Selecting high-quality, grade-appropriate passages requires both objective text 
complexity metrics and expert judgment. For the Grades 3–8 assessments based on the 
New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards for English Language Arts, both 
quantitative and qualitative rubrics are used to determine the complexity of the texts and 
their appropriate placement within a grade-level ELA exam.
Quantitative measures of text complexity are used to measure aspects of text complexity 
that are difficult for a human reader to evaluate when examining a text. These aspects 
include word frequency, word length, sentence length, and text cohesion. These aspects 
are efficiently measured by computer programs. While quantitative text complexity 
metrics are a helpful start, they are not definitive.
Qualitative measures are a crucial complement to quantitative measures. Using 
qualitative measures of text complexity involves making an informed decision about the 
difficulty of a text in terms of one or more factors discernible to a human reader applying 
trained judgment to the task. To qualitatively determine the complexity of a text, NYS 
educators use a rubric composed of five factors; four of these factors are required and 
one factor is optional. The required criteria are: meaning, text structure, language 
features, and knowledge demands. The optional factor, graphics, is used only if a graphic 
appears in the text.
To make the final determination as to whether a text is at grade-level and thus 
appropriate to be included on a Grades 3–8 assessment, New York State uses a two-step 
review process, which is an industry best-practice. First, all prospective passages undergo 
quantitative text complexity analysis using three text complexity measures. If at least two 
of the three measures suggest that the passage is grade-appropriate, the passage then 
moves to the second step, which is the qualitative review using the text-complexity 
rubrics. Only passages that are determined appropriate by at least two of three 
quantitative measures of complexity and are determined appropriate by the qualitative 
measure of complexity are deemed appropriate for use on the exam.

Background
As in past years, SED is releasing large portions of the 2024 NYS Grades 3–8 English Language Arts and
Mathematics test materials for review, discussion, and use.
For 2024, included in these released materials are at least 75 percent of the test questions that appeared on 
the 2024 tests (including all constructed-response questions) that counted toward students’ scores. 
Additionally, SED is also providing a map that details what each released question measures and the correct 
response to each question. These released materials will help students, families, educators, and the public 
better understand the tests and the New York State Education Department’s expectations for students.
Understanding Math Questions
Multiple-Choice Questions
Multiple-choice questions are designed to assess the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning 
Standards for Mathematics. Mathematics multiple-choice questions will be used mainly to assess standard 
algorithms and conceptual standards. Multiple-choice questions incorporate both the grade-level standards 
and the “Standards for Mathematical Practices.” Many questions are framed within the context of real-world 
applications or require students to complete multiple steps. Likewise, many of these questions are linked to 
more than one standard, drawing on the simultaneous application of multiple skills and concepts.
One-Credit Constructed-Response Questions
One-credit constructed-response questions require students to complete a task and provide only their final 
answer. These one-credit questions will often require multiple steps, assessing procedural skills, as well as 
conceptual understanding and application. While students may show how they arrived at their final answer, 
only the final answer will be scored.
Two-Credit Constructed-Response Questions
Two-credit constructed-response questions require students to complete tasks and show their work. These
two-credit response questions will often require multiple steps, the application of multiple mathematics 
skills, and real-world applications. Many of the short-response questions will cover conceptual and 
application standards.
Three-Credit Constructed-Response Questions
Three-credit constructed-response questions ask students to show their work in completing two or more 
tasks or a more extensive problem. These three-credit response questions allow students to show their 
understanding of mathematical procedures, conceptual understanding, and application. Three-credit 
response questions may also assess student reasoning and the ability to critique the arguments of others. The 
scoring rubric for all constructed-response questions can be found in the grade-level Educator Guides at 
https://www.nysed.gov/state-assessment/grades-3-8-ela-and-math-test-manuals.
New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards Alignment 
The alignment(s) to the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards for Mathematics is/are 
intended to identify the primary analytic skills necessary to successfully answer each question. However, 
some questions measure proficiencies described in multiple standards, including a balanced combination of 
procedure and conceptual understanding. For example, two-credit and three-credit constructed-response 
questions require students to show an understanding of mathematical procedures, concepts, and 
applications. 
These Released Questions Do Not Comprise a “Mini Test” 
To ensure it is possible to develop future tests, some content must remain secure. This document is not
intended to be representative of the entire test, to show how operational tests look, or to provide 
information about how teachers should administer the test; rather, its purpose is to provide an overview of 
how the test reflects the demands of the New York State P–12 Next Generation Learning Standards.
The released questions do not represent the full spectrum of the standards assessed on the State tests, nor 
do they represent the full spectrum of how the standards should be taught and assessed in the classroom. It 
should not be assumed that a particular standard will be measured by an identical question in future 
assessments.

Lighthouses were once lit by gas. A gas lighthouse on the Hudson River
closed and later became a park after the George Washington Bridge was
built.

Excerpt from The Little Red
Lighthouse and the Great Gray Bridge

by Hildegarde H. Swift and Lynd Ward

Once upon a time a little lighthouse was built on a sharp point of the
shore by the Hudson River... .

Behind it lay New York City where the people lived.

Before it sailed the boats on which the people rode. Up and down, up
and down, sailed the boats. On and on and on rolled the river. All the way
from Lake Tear of the Clouds, high up in the mountains, came the Hudson
River. It rolled down the mountains. It rolled and rolled and rolled. It rolled
past Albany. It rolled past New York. And it went on forever looking for the
sea.

Now the boats on the river talked to the little red lighthouse as they
passed.

“Hoot, hoot, hoot! How are you?” said the big steamer, with its deep,
throaty whistle. . ..

By day the little red lighthouse did not answer. ...

But every night, just at fall of dark, a man came to tend the little red
lighthouse. He took out his jingling keys. He unlocked the small red door in
its side. He climbed its steep and winding stairs, up, up, up, to the very top.
He took off the thick white cap that let it sleep by day. He turned on the gas
with a funny small black key.

Up, up, up, flowed the gas from the six red tanks below.

Page 2

11
12

19

Then the little red lighthouse spoke out plainly. Flash! Flash! Flash! . ..

It felt big and useful and important. What would the boats do without
me? it thought.

It felt VERY, VERY PROUD.

The boats saw the light and were safe. The boats saw it, and they kept to
the channel. The boats were grateful to the little red lighthouse. . . .

channel = deepest part of a river or harbor

Why, I am MASTER OF THE RIVER, it thought.
Then one day a gang of workmen came and began to dig. ...

Every day it watched the strange new gray thing beside it grow and
grow. Huge towers seemed to touch the sky. Strong loops of steel swept
across the river. How big it was! How wonderful! How powerful! A great
gray bridge, spanning the Hudson River from shore to shore. It made the
little red lighthouse feel very, very small.

spanning = going across

Then one night a great beam of light flashed from the top of the nearest
gray tower....

Now I am needed no longer, thought the little red lighthouse. My light
is so little and this one so big!...

Then in the middle of the night there came a storm. The wind moaned.
The waves beat against the shore. ...

Then the great gray bridge called to the little red lighthouse: “Little
brother, where is your light?”

Released Questions with Annotations
With the adoption of the New York P–12 Common Core Learning Standards (CCLS) in ELA/Literacy and 
Mathematics, the Board of Regents signaled a shift in both instruction and assessment. Starting in Spring 2013, 
New York State began administering tests designed to assess student performance in accordance with the 
instructional shifts and the rigor demanded by the Common Core State Standards (CCSS). To aid in the 
transition to new assessments, New York State has released a number of resources, including test blueprints 
and specifications, sample questions, and criteria for writing assessment questions. These resources can be 
found at http://www.engageny.org/common-core-assessments. 
New York State administered the ELA/Literacy and Mathematics Common Core tests in April 2014 and is now 
making a portion of the questions from those tests available for review and use. These released questions will 
help students, families, educators, and the public better understand how tests have changed to assess the 
instructional shifts demanded by the Common Core and to assess the rigor required to ensure that all students 
are on track to college and career readiness.
Annotated Questions Are Teaching Tools
The released questions are intended to help educators, students, families, and the public understand how the 
Common Core is different. The annotated questions demonstrate the way the Common Core should drive 
instruction and how tests have changed to better assess student performance in accordance with the 
instructional shifts demanded by the Common Core. They are also intended to help educators identify how 
the rigor of the State tests can inform classroom instruction and local assessment. To this end, these annotated 
questions will include instructional suggestions for mastery of the CCLS. 
The annotated questions will include both multiple-choice and constructed-response questions. With each 
multiple-choice question released, a rationale will be available to demonstrate why the question measures 
the intended standards; why the correct answer is correct; and why each wrong answer is plausible but 
incorrect. Additionally, for each constructed-response question, there will be an explanation for why the 
question measures the intended standards and an annotated rubric with sample student responses that 
would obtain each score on the rubric. 
Understanding ELA Annotated Questions
Multiple Choice
Multiple-choice questions are designed to assess Common Core Reading and Language Standards. They will 
ask students to analyze different aspects of a given text, including central idea, style elements, character and 
plot development, and vocabulary. Almost all questions, including vocabulary questions, will only be 
answered correctly if the student comprehends and makes use of the whole passage. For multiple-choice 
questions, students will select the correct response from four answer choices. 
Multiple-choice questions will assess Reading Standards in a range of ways. Some will ask students to analyze 
aspects of text or vocabulary. Many questions will require students to combine skills. For example, questions 
may ask students to identify a segment of text that best supports the central idea. To answer correctly, a 
student must first comprehend the central idea and then show understanding of how that idea is supported. 
Questions will require more than rote recall or identification. Students will also be required to negotiate 
plausible, text-based distractors1. Each distractor will require students to comprehend the whole passage.
The rationales describe why the distractors are plausible but incorrect and are based in common 
misconceptions regarding the text. While these rationales will speak to a possible and likely reason for 
selection of the incorrect option by the student, these rationales do not contain definitive statements as to why 
the student chose the incorrect option or what we can infer about knowledge and skills of the student based 
on their selection of an incorrect response. These multiple-choice questions were designed to assess student 
proficiency, not to diagnose specific misconceptions/errors with each and every incorrect option.
The annotations accompanying the multiple-choice questions will also include instructional suggestions for 
mastery of the CCLS measured.
Short Response
Short-response questions are designed to assess Common Core Reading and Language Standards. These are 
single questions in which students use textual evidence to support their own answer to an inferential question. 
These questions ask the student to make an inference (a claim, position, or conclusion) based on his or her 
analysis of the passage, and then provide two pieces of text-based evidence to support his or her answer. 
The purpose of the short-response questions is to assess a student’s ability to comprehend and analyze text. In 
responding to these questions, students will be expected to write in complete sentences. Responses should 
require no more than three complete sentences
The rubric used for evaluating short-response questions can be found both in the grade-level annotations and 
in the Educator Guide to the 2014 Grade 3 Common Core English Language Arts Test at 
www.engageny.org/resource/test-guides-for-english-language-arts-and-mathematics.
Extended Response
Extended-response questions are designed to measure a student’s ability to Write from Sources. Questions 
that measure Writing from Sources prompt students to communicate a clear and coherent analysis of one or 
two texts. The comprehension and analysis required by each extended response is directly related to grade 
specific reading standards.
Student responses are evaluated on the degree to which they meet grade-level writing and language 
expectations. This evaluation is made using a rubric that incorporates the demands of grade specific Common 
Core Writing, Reading, and Language standards. The integrated nature of the Common Core Learning 
Standards for ELA and Literacy require that students are evaluated across the strands (Reading, Writing, and 
Language) with longer piece of writing such as those prompted by the extended-response questions. 
The information in the annotated extended-responses questions focuses on the demands of the questions and 
as such will show how the question measures the Common Core Reading standards. 
The rubric used for evaluating extended-response can be found both in the grade-level annotations and in the 
Educator Guide to the 2014 Grade 3 Common Core English Language Arts Test at 
www.engageny.org/resource/test-guides-for-english-language-arts-and-mathematics.
These Released Questions Do Not Comprise a Mini Test
1 A distractor is an incorrect response that may appear to be a plausible correct response to a student who 
has not mastered the skill or concept being tested.
This document is NOT intended to show how operational tests look or to provide information about how 
teachers should administer the test; rather, its purpose is to provide an overview of how the new test reflects 
the demand of the CCSS. 
The released questions do not represent the full spectrum of standards assessed on the State tests, nor do they 
represent the full spectrum of how the Common Core should be taught and assessed in the classroom. 
Specific criteria for writing test questions as well as additional assessment information is available at 
www.engageny.org/common-core-assessments.

1 You probably like to eat maple syrup

2 It is true. Maple syrup is made from

3 When the weather is cold, sap is stored
in the tree’s roots. But when the weather
starts to warm up, sap begins to move up
and down in the tree. This usually
happens in March, when spring weather
begins.
4 On warm spring days, sap flows to the
branches, where it makes leaves bud. On
cool spring nights, sap goes back down to
the roots. When the sap is flowing up and
down in the tree for many days in a row,
syrup makers start collecting it. Sugaring
season has begun.
Tapping the Trees
5 Sap flows inside the tree. Syrup makers
drill small holes in the tree to collect the
sap. They call this tapping the tree.
6 After syrup makers tap the tree, they
put a spout into each hole.
Directions
1
7 Some syrup makers hang buckets beneath the spouts to collect the sap.
When the buckets are full, they empty the sap into a gathering tank, which
is taken to the sugarhouse.
8 Other syrup makers use plastic tubing to collect the sap. The tubing
connects to the spout and sap flows through the tubing into storage tanks
near the sugarhouse.
In the Sugarhouse
9 Sap is mostly water. To make maple syrup, the water must be removed.
This is done inside the sugarhouse.
10 Sap is poured into large pans; then it is boiled. Boiling takes out the
water, or makes it evaporate. Pure maple syrup is left behind.
Filtering
11 Before the syrup can be bottled, it must be filtered to remove the “sugar
sand.” This is a gritty substance made of minerals from the maple tree.
Color Grading
12 After the syrup is filtered, it is graded by color:
• Grade A—Light Amber (Fancy)
• Grade A—Medium Amber
• Grade A—Dark Amber
• Grade B—(darkest of all)
13 The best syrup to buy is the one you like the most. The darker the color,
the stronger the flavor.
Bottling It Up
14 Now it is time to put the syrup into bottles. These bottles of maple syrup
will be sent to grocery stores all around the world.
Time to Rest
15 When the days and nights stay warm, the tree leaves start to bud. When
this happens the sap is not as sweet. And that means the maple syrup will
not be as sweet, either. So syrup makers stop collecting sap. Sugaring season
is over—until next year.

# Research Report: NYS Grade 3 ELA and Math Released Test Questions

## Executive Summary

Based on comprehensive analysis of official NYSED released test materials from 2024 and 2025 (with supplemental annotated items from 2014), this report documents the exact format, question types, passage types, answer labeling conventions, question stem wording patterns, scoring rubrics, and full example questions from the New York State Grade 3 ELA and Mathematics assessments.

### Key Findings

1. **Answer choices use A/B/C/D labeling** (not 1/2/3/4) for all multiple-choice questions in both ELA and Math.
2. **ELA passages are a mix of literary (fiction/poetry) and informational (nonfiction/science)** -- typically 2 literary and 2 informational per test, with word counts of 500-640 words and Lexile ranges of 530-620.
3. **Constructed-response questions in ELA are always 2-credit items** requiring an inference plus two pieces of text-based evidence in no more than 3 complete sentences. Math CR questions come in 1-credit (answer only), 2-credit (show work + explain), and 3-credit (multi-part extended) varieties.
4. **Question stems follow highly predictable wording patterns** -- ELA uses phrases like "Which detail best shows...", "What does [word] mean as it is used in paragraph X?", "Use two details from the passage to support your response."
5. **The 2014 annotated items provide explicit rationales** for why correct answers are correct AND why each incorrect answer is plausible but wrong, plus instructional guidance for each standard measured.

---

## SECTION 1: TEST STRUCTURE AND FORMAT

### ELA Test Structure (Grade 3)
- **Two sessions** (Session 1 and Session 2)
- **Approximately 75% of questions released** each year (all CR questions included)
- Each session contains 1-2 reading passages followed by questions
- Total released questions per year: approximately 20-24 (mix of MC and CR)
- **Passage types per test:**
  - 2025: "Excerpt from Sea View Star" (literary), "Raccoons" (informational), "Excerpt from Big Moon Tortilla" (literary), "The North Wind and the Sun" (poem/literary)
  - 2024: "Excerpt from Crazy About Clouds" (informational), "Excerpt from The Little Red Lighthouse and the Great Gray Bridge" (literary), "Apple Day" (literary), "Excerpt from Hide with the Ladybugs" (informational)

### Math Test Structure (Grade 3)
- **Two sessions** (Session 1 and Session 2)
- Session 1: Multiple-choice only (no calculator, ruler available)
- Session 2: Multiple-choice PLUS constructed-response questions
- CR questions span 1-credit, 2-credit, and 3-credit levels

### Answer Choice Labeling
**All multiple-choice questions use A, B, C, D labeling** in both ELA and Math.

---

## SECTION 2: ELA RELEASED QUESTIONS WITH FULL DETAIL

### 2025 ELA -- Session 1, Passage 1: "Excerpt from Sea View Star" by Ali Bovis (LITERARY)
- Word Count: 640, Lexile: 610, Flesch-Kincaid: 3.5, ATOS: 3.7
- Synopsis: Sylvie and Sammy plan a "Paint Out" art show fundraiser for an animal shelter on Make a Difference Day.

**Question 1** (Standard: NY-3.R.4 -- Vocabulary)
> In paragraph 7, what does Sylvie mean when she says, "Sounds more stellar, doesn't it?"

- A. She wants people to think the event will be amazing. **(CORRECT -- 48% of students answered correctly)**
- B. She wants people to be sure art is part of the event.
- C. She wants people to bring their own paint to the event.
- D. She wants people to know the event will help animals.

**Question 2** (Standard: NY-3.R.3 -- Character/Plot Development)
> What do the details in paragraphs 2 and 9 show about Sylvie?

- A. She likes playing on the swings.
- B. She cares about animals. **(CORRECT -- 82%)**
- C. She knows a lot about the town.
- D. She is a talented artist.

**Question 3** (Standard: NY-3.R.3 -- Character/Plot Development)
> Based on paragraph 14, why does Sammy make a confused face in paragraph 15?

- A. He is surprised that Sylvie thinks it is getting late. **(CORRECT -- 71%)**
- B. He is disappointed that Sylvie was ready before him.
- C. He is worried his parents did not pack everything.
- D. He is not sure why they need a booth for the dog.

**Question 4** (Standard: NY-3.R.9 -- Text Connections)
> "Preparation is the key to success" is a common saying that means when you plan for an event, it is more likely to go well. Which paragraph best shows how this idea is demonstrated in the story?

- A. paragraph 1
- B. paragraph 3
- C. paragraph 11 **(CORRECT -- 57%)**
- D. paragraph 15

**Question 5** (Standard: NY-3.R.2 -- Central Idea)
> Which detail best expresses a central idea of the story?

- A. " 'Hmm.' Sammy tilted his head. 'I have a few ideas too.' " (paragraph 3)
- B. "Then we can sell the paintings. We can donate the money to the Sea View Animal Shelter." (paragraph 9) **(CORRECT -- 68%)**
- C. "Sylvie looked around. A balloon arch spelled 'Happy Make a Difference Day' from streetlight to streetlight." (paragraph 19)
- D. "Sylvie nodded. She squeezed in a few warm-up stretches and reached for her megaphone." (paragraph 26)

**Question 6** (Standard: NY-3.R.6 -- Point of View/Author's Purpose)
> By the end of the story, how does Sylvie most likely feel about the Paint Out?

- A. She is relieved that the planning and work are finished.
- B. She is upset because she did most of the work.
- C. She is concerned that they did not raise enough money.
- D. She is proud that the event was a huge success. **(CORRECT -- 74%)**

---

### 2025 ELA -- Session 1, Passage 2: "Raccoons" by Meryl Magby (INFORMATIONAL)
- Word Count: 571, Lexile: 620, Flesch-Kincaid: 4.5, ATOS: 5.3
- Informational text about raccoon biology, behavior, and habitat
- Includes section headings: "Flexible Fingers," "Eating Almost Anything," "Night and Day," "Many Different Dens," "People and Raccoons"
- Includes graphic/illustration of raccoon with labeled features

**Question 19** (Standard: NY-3.R.7 -- Graphics/Illustrations)
> How does the graphic support the information in paragraph 2?

- A. by showing what raccoons look like **(CORRECT -- 74%)**
- B. by explaining that raccoons have good hearing
- C. by explaining that raccoons can see at night
- D. by showing where some raccoons live

**Question 20** (Standard: NY-3.R.3 -- Key Details)
> Which sentence best explains why raccoons mostly come out at night?

- A. Raccoons find their food when it is dark.
- B. Raccoons spend most of their time alone.
- C. Raccoons hide during the day to keep safe. **(CORRECT -- 36%)**
- D. Raccoons have black masks and tail rings.

**Question 21** (Standard: NY-3.R.5 -- Text Structure)
> In which section would the reader find information about what raccoons do during the winter?

- A. "Flexible Fingers"
- B. "Eating Almost Anything"
- C. "Night and Day"
- D. "Many Different Dens" **(CORRECT -- 60%)**

**Question 22** (Standard: NY-3.R.9 -- Text Connections)
> Animals learn to do things a certain way because of their habitat. Which sentence from the passage best connects to this idea?

- A. "Raccoon paws have five fingers, just like a person's hands do." (paragraph 2)
- B. "Exactly what a raccoon eats depends on where it lives." (paragraph 4) **(CORRECT -- 41%)**
- C. "They use the many nerves on the bottoms of their paws to feel their food." (paragraph 5)
- D. "In this home range, they have many hiding places that they use as dens." (paragraph 8)

**Question 23** (Standard: NY-3.R.6 -- Author's Purpose/Perspective)
> Which detail from the passage best shows what the author thinks about raccoons?

- A. "Raccoons are very smart and quick." (paragraph 3) **(CORRECT -- 67%)**
- B. "Raccoons living near people also eat pet food . . ." (paragraph 4)
- C. "Raccoons often dip their food in water . . ." (paragraph 5)
- D. "Raccoons can sometimes spread diseases to people." (paragraph 11)

**Question 24** (Standard: NY-3.R.4 -- Vocabulary; 2-CREDIT CONSTRUCTED RESPONSE)
> What does "pests" mean as it is used in paragraph 6? Use two details from the passage to support your response.
>
> (Average points earned: 1.06 out of 2 = 53%)

**Question 25** (Standard: NY-3.R.2 -- Central Idea; 2-CREDIT CONSTRUCTED RESPONSE)
> What is a central idea of the passage? Use two details from the passage to support your response.
>
> (Average points earned: 1.20 out of 2 = 60%)

---

### 2025 ELA -- Session 2, Passage 1: "Excerpt from Big Moon Tortilla" by Joy Cowley (LITERARY)
- Word Count: 607, Lexile: 590, Flesch-Kincaid: 4.0, ATOS: 4.7

**Question 26** (Standard: NY-3.R.5 -- Text Structure)
> Why is the information in paragraph 3 important to the story?

- A. It shows the cause of a problem in the story. **(CORRECT -- 77%)**
- B. It gives details about the setting.
- C. It gives a hint about how the story will end.
- D. It introduces a new character.

**Question 27** (Standard: NY-3.L.4 -- Language/Vocabulary)
> Which meaning of the word "spread" is used in paragraph 5?

- A. scattered around **(CORRECT -- 74%)**
- B. grew slowly
- C. became known
- D. shared freely

**Question 28** (Standard: NY-3.R.3 -- Key Details)
> Which detail from the story best tells why Marta begins crying?

- A. "It went to Marta's stomach . . ." (paragraph 2)
- B. "They were in such a hurry to run to the cookhouse that they knocked over Marta's table . . ." (paragraph 3)
- C. "But the dogs, too, were chasing. Leaping into the air, they barked to each other. A game!" (paragraph 7)
- D. "Ruined homework! Broken glasses!" (paragraph 10) **(CORRECT -- 78%)**

**Question 29** (Standard: NY-3.R.8 -- Reasoning/Evidence)
> Which detail from the story best tells what Grandmother believes?

- A. "Grandmother's big moon tortillas were the best in the world!" (paragraph 1)
- B. "When we have a problem we must choose what we will be." (paragraph 16) **(CORRECT -- 50%)**
- C. "When the eagle is high up, it sees how small the earth is." (paragraph 19)
- D. "Grandmother wound some tape around the arm of the glasses." (paragraph 20)

**Question 30** (Standard: NY-3.R.6 -- Point of View)
> What does Marta most likely think about Grandmother's healing song?

- A. It reminds her of a story.
- B. It helps her make a choice. **(CORRECT -- 51%)**
- C. It makes her want to dance.
- D. It teaches her about animals.

**Question 31** (Standard: NY-3.R.2 -- Central Idea/Summary)
> Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story?

- A. Marta's stomach makes loud noises.
- B. The tortillas are in the cookhouse.
- C. The tortillas bubble and turn brown.
- D. Marta decides to be like an eagle. **(CORRECT -- 54%)**

---

### 2025 ELA -- Session 2, Passage 2: "The North Wind and the Sun" by Mary Ann Hoberman (POEM/LITERARY)
- Word Count: 213
- A poem in dialogue form (fable retelling) where the North Wind and the Sun compete

**Question 32** (2-CREDIT CONSTRUCTED RESPONSE, Standard: NY-3.R.3)
> In "The North Wind and the Sun," what do lines 9 through 12 show about the North Wind? Use two details from the poem to support your response.
>
> (Average: 1.04/2 = 52%)

**Question 33** (2-CREDIT CONSTRUCTED RESPONSE, Standard: NY-3.R.6)
> In "The North Wind and the Sun," how does the Sun feel about having power? Use two details from the poem to support your response.
>
> (Average: 1.20/2 = 60%)

**Question 34** (2-CREDIT CONSTRUCTED RESPONSE, Standard: NY-3.R.3)

## SECTION 4: SCORING RUBRICS

### ELA 2-Credit Constructed-Response Rubric

**2 Credits** -- The features of a 2-credit response are:
- Valid inferences and/or claims from the text where required by the prompt
- Evidence of analysis of the text where required by the prompt
- Relevant facts, definitions, concrete details, and/or other information from the text to develop response according to the requirements of the prompt
- Sufficient number of facts, definitions, concrete details, and/or other information from the text as required by the prompt
- Complete sentences where errors do not impact readability

**1 Credit** -- The features of a 1-credit response are:
- A mostly literal recounting of events or details from the text as required by the prompt
- Some relevant facts, definitions, concrete details, and/or other information from the text to develop response according to the requirements of the prompt
- Incomplete sentences or bullets

**0 Credits** -- The features of a 0-credit response are:
- A response that does not address any of the requirements of the prompt or is totally inaccurate
- A response that is not written in English
- A response that is unintelligible or indecipherable

**Special Rule:** If the prompt requires two texts and the student only references one text, the response can be scored no higher than a 1.

### Math Constructed-Response Scoring (from 2025 Scoring Leader Materials)

**1-Credit Questions:** Only the final answer is scored (correct = 1, incorrect = 0).

**2-Credit Questions:**
- **2 Credits:** "This response demonstrates a thorough understanding of the mathematical concepts and procedures in the task." Correct answer given AND supported by correct work/explanation.
- **1 Credit:** "This response demonstrates only a partial understanding..." Correct answer but incomplete explanation, OR incorrect answer with correct procedures shown.
- **0 Credits:** "This response is not sufficient to demonstrate even a limited understanding..." Incorrect answer with no valid work, OR correct answer with no work shown at all.

**Critical Scoring Policies (from scoring materials):**
- **Scoring Policy #3:** For 2- and 3-credit responses, a correct answer with NO work shown receives 0 credits.
- **Scoring Policy #10:** For 2- and 3-credit responses, number sentences (equations) must be written horizontally (vertical format loses credit).

**3-Credit Questions:**
- **3 Credits:** All parts correct with supporting work
- **2 Credits:** Most parts correct with some errors
- **1 Credit:** Only some elements correct
- **0 Credits:** Insufficient understanding, OR correct answers with no work shown (Policy #3)

## SECTION 5: ANNOTATED EXAMPLES (from 2014 Released Items)

The 2014 annotated items provide the most detailed explanations of WHY answers are correct. Here are key examples from the "Sugaring Time" informational passage:

### Annotated Example 1 -- Illustration/Text Feature Question
**Standard: RI.3.5** (Use text features to locate information)

> "The first illustration best helps the reader understand the information in which paragraph?"
- A. paragraph 2
- B. paragraph 4 **(CORRECT)**
- C. paragraph 5
- D. paragraph 7

**Why B is correct:** "Students who choose 'B' are able to recognize how the illustration relates to the ideas described in paragraph 4. The illustration shows the flow of the sap moving up toward the branches in the daytime and back toward the roots at night, which mirrors the way paragraph 4 describes the movement of sap during day and night."

**Why A is wrong:** "Students may have chosen 'A' because the illustration shows how sap flows in the trees, which is implied in paragraph 2. However, the text in paragraph 2 contains no information that maps directly to the sun, moon, and arrows."

**Why C is wrong:** "Students may have chosen 'C' because paragraph 5 mentions the movement of the sap, which the illustration captures, but the text does not explain the importance of day and night."

**Why D is wrong:** "Students may have chosen 'D' because paragraph 7 indicates the time and location for the placement of the buckets. However, the text focuses on what syrup makers do rather than the natural processes portrayed in the illustration."

### Annotated Example 2 -- Sequence/Steps Question
**Standard: RI.3.3** (Sequence of steps in technical procedures)

> "Read the chart below. [SUGARING STEPS: 1. Syrup makers tap trees. 2. _____. 3. Syrup makers collect sap in buckets.] Which statement best completes the chart?"
- A. Syrup makers store syrup in tanks.
- B. Syrup makers boil sap in large pans.
- C. Syrup makers put spouts into drilled holes. **(CORRECT)**
- D. Syrup makers pour sap into gathering tanks.

**Why C is correct:** "Students who choose 'C' use the text to identify the steps of the sap-making process in the correct order. In order to correctly identify the omitted step in the chart, the students must comprehend the processes involved in each step and place these steps in appropriate order."

**Why D is wrong:** "Students may have chosen 'D' because the text presents a sequential relationship between collecting the sap in buckets and pouring it into the tanks. This choice reverses the order of this process rather than describing the process of collecting sap in sequential order."

### Annotated Example 3 -- Explicit Information Question
**Standard: RI.3.1** (Answer questions referring explicitly to text)

> "According to the Filtering section, why do syrup makers remove minerals from maple syrup?"
- A. Minerals make the syrup grainy. **(CORRECT)**
- B. Minerals change the syrup's taste.
- C. Minerals change the syrup's color.
- D. Minerals stop the syrup from flowing.

**Why A is correct:** "Students who choose 'A' have successfully identified that minerals change the consistency of syrup and that this change is the reason syrup makers want to remove minerals from the syrup."

**Why B is wrong:** "Students may have chosen 'B' because substances in the sap could have a negative effect on the syrup's taste. The text may imply this connection, but it is not stated explicitly."

## SECTION 6: QUESTION STEM WORDING PATTERNS

### ELA Multiple-Choice Stem Patterns (recurring across 2024-2025)

**Vocabulary/Word Meaning:**
- "What does [word] mean as it is used in paragraph X?"
- "Which meaning of the word [word] is used in paragraph X?"
- "In paragraph X, what does [character] mean when she/he says, '[quote]'?"
- "The phrase '[phrase]' in paragraph X suggests that..."

**Central Idea/Theme:**
- "Which detail best expresses a central idea of the story?"
- "Which sentence best shows a theme of the story?"
- "What is a central idea of the passage?"
- "Which detail would be most important to include in a summary of the story/passage?"

**Character/Plot Development:**
- "What do the details in paragraphs X and Y show about [character]?"
- "By the end of the story, how does [character] most likely feel about...?"
- "Which sentence best shows how [character]'s feelings about [other character] change in the story?"
- "Based on paragraph X, why does [character] [action] in paragraph Y?"

**Text Structure:**
- "Why is the information in paragraph X important to the story?"
- "How are the details in paragraphs X through Y important to the story?"
- "In which section would the reader find information about...?"
- "In which section of the passage can the reader find information about...?"

**Author's Purpose/Perspective:**
- "Which detail best shows how the author feels about [topic]?"
- "Which detail from the passage best shows what the author thinks about [topic]?"

**Cross-Text/Real-World Connections:**
- "[Statement about a general principle]. Which paragraph/detail best shows how this idea is demonstrated in the story?"
- "[General claim about topic]. Which detail best shows how this idea connects to the events in the story?"
- "Animals learn to do things a certain way because of their habitat. Which sentence from the passage best connects to this idea?"

**Evidence/Reasoning:**
- "Which detail from the story best tells what [character] believes?"
- "What does [character] most likely think about [event/element]?"
- "Which sentence from the passage shows a cause-and-effect relationship?"
- "Which quote from the story best shows that [character] has learned a lesson?"
- "In paragraph X of the story, whose thoughts are being shown?"

**Graphics/Illustrations:**
- "How does the graphic support the information in paragraph X?"
- "How does the diagram [title] better help the reader's understanding of the passage?"
- "How does the illustration add to the reader's understanding of [title]?"

### ELA Constructed-Response Stem Patterns

ALL follow this template:
> "[Specific analytical question about the passage]? Use two details from the passage/story/poem to support your response."

Examples:
- "What does 'pests' mean as it is used in paragraph 6? Use two details from the passage to support your response."
- "What is a central idea of the passage? Use two details from the passage to support your response."
- "In [Title], what do lines X through Y show about [character]? Use two details from the poem to support your response."
- "How does paragraph X connect to paragraph Y? Use two details from the passage to support your response."

### Math Question Stem Patterns

**Expression/Equation Matching:**
- "Which expression represents how to find...?"
- "Which set of equations can be used to find...?"
- "Which equation can be used to solve for the unknown?"
- "Which expression is equivalent to...?"
- "Which expression cannot be used to find the area...?"

**Fraction Questions:**
- "Which fraction has a value equivalent to [X]?"
- "Which fraction is represented by point P on the number line?"
- "Which two fractions each have a value greater than [X]?"
- "Which two fractions are equivalent?"
- "Which number sentence is true?" (fraction comparisons)

**Area/Measurement:**
- "What is the area, in square units, of the rectangle?"
- "What is the total area, in square feet, of the [shape]?"
- "How many unit squares are needed to cover a rectangle with an area of X square units?"

**Place Value/Rounding:**
- "What digit is in the [place] place in the number X?"
- "What is the number X rounded to the nearest [place]?"
- "Write the number X in expanded form."

**Word Problems:**
- "Which story problem can be represented by the expression [X]?"
- "Which value is closest to the number of [units]...?"

**Math CR Stem Patterns:**
- "Show your work." / "Explain how you found your answer."
- "Be sure to include how place value or groups of ten can be used to find your answer."
- "Be sure to include what you know about fractions or parts of a whole in your answer."
- "Be sure to include the area, in square feet, of each [item] in your answer."
- "Explain how you know your answer is correct."

---

## SECTION 7: PASSAGE TYPES AND TEXT COMPLEXITY

### ELA Passage Types Used (2024-2025)

| Year | Passage | Type | Genre | Word Count | Lexile |
|------|---------|------|-------|------------|--------|
| 2025 | Excerpt from Sea View Star | Literary | Realistic Fiction | 640 | 610 |
| 2025 | Raccoons | Informational | Science/Nature | 571 | 620 |
| 2025 | Excerpt from Big Moon Tortilla | Literary | Realistic Fiction (multicultural) | 607 | 590 |
| 2025 | The North Wind and the Sun | Literary | Poem (fable) | 213 | n/a |
| 2024 | Excerpt from Crazy About Clouds | Informational | Science/Nature | 509 | 550 |
| 2024 | The Little Red Lighthouse... | Literary | Fiction (personification) | 618 | 530 |
| 2024 | Apple Day | Literary | Realistic Fiction | 528 | 580 |
| 2024 | Hide with the Ladybugs | Informational | Science/Nature | 580 | 550 |

**Key observations:**
- Consistent mix of literary (fiction, poem) and informational (science/nature) texts
- Word counts range from 213 (poem) to 640 words
- Lexile range: 530-620 (Grade 2-3 band: 420-820)
- Literary passages are typically excerpted from published children's books
- Informational passages use section headings, graphics, and sidebars
- Informational passages often include glossed vocabulary (definitions in margins)

# NYS Grade 3 ELA and Math Released Test Questions -- Research Report

## Executive Summary

I crawled and analyzed the official NYSED released test materials from 2024, 2025, the 2025 Math Scoring Leader Materials, the 2024 ELA Scoring Leader Materials, and the 2014 Annotated Items document. Here are the five most important findings:

1. **Answer choices always use A/B/C/D** -- never 1/2/3/4, in both ELA and Math.
2. **Passage types are a balanced mix**: each test year includes approximately 2 literary texts (realistic fiction, fable/poem, personification story) and 2 informational texts (science/nature with section headings, diagrams, and glossed vocabulary). Word counts range from 213 (poem) to 640.
3. **ELA constructed-response questions are always 2-credit items** with an invariant stem pattern: an analytical question followed by "Use two details from the passage/story/poem to support your response." Students must write no more than 3 complete sentences containing an inference plus two pieces of textual evidence.
4. **Math constructed-response questions come in three tiers**: 1-credit (answer only), 2-credit (show work AND explain), and 3-credit (multi-part). A critical scoring policy is that correct answers with NO work shown receive 0 credits on 2- and 3-credit items.
5. **Question stems follow highly predictable patterns** documented in detail in Section 6 of the report.

---

## 1. Answer Choice Labeling

Every multiple-choice question across both subjects in both years uses **A, B, C, D**. There are no exceptions. Four answer choices are always provided.

---

## 2. Passage Types Used

### 2025 ELA Passages
| Passage | Type | Word Count | Lexile |
|---------|------|-----------|--------|
| Excerpt from Sea View Star | Literary (realistic fiction) | 640 | 610 |
| Raccoons | Informational (science/nature) | 571 | 620 |
| Excerpt from Big Moon Tortilla | Literary (multicultural fiction) | 607 | 590 |
| The North Wind and the Sun | Literary (poem/fable) | 213 | n/a |

### 2024 ELA Passages
| Passage | Type | Word Count | Lexile |
|---------|------|-----------|--------|
| Excerpt from Crazy About Clouds | Informational (science) | 509 | 550 |
| The Little Red Lighthouse and the Great Gray Bridge | Literary (personification) | 618 | 530 |
| Apple Day | Literary (realistic fiction) | 528 | 580 |
| Excerpt from Hide with the Ladybugs | Informational (science/nature) | 580 | 550 |

**Informational texts** consistently include: section headings, sidebars, diagrams/illustrations, and glossed vocabulary in margins. **Literary texts** include fiction excerpts from published children's books and at least one poem per year.

---

## 3. Actual Released Multiple-Choice Questions (ELA)

Below are complete examples with correct answers marked. The full set (40+ questions across both years) is in the plan file.

### Example Literary MC Questions (2025, "Sea View Star")

**Q1** (Vocabulary, NY-3.R.4): *In paragraph 7, what does Sylvie mean when she says, "Sounds more stellar, doesn't it?"*
- **A. She wants people to think the event will be amazing.** (CORRECT, 48%)
- B. She wants people to be sure art is part of the event.
- C. She wants people to bring their own paint to the event.
- D. She wants people to know the event will help animals.

**Q5** (Central Idea, NY-3.R.2): *Which detail best expresses a central idea of the story?*
- A. " 'Hmm.' Sammy tilted his head. 'I have a few ideas too.' " (paragraph 3)
- **B. "Then we can sell the paintings. We can donate the money to the Sea View Animal Shelter." (paragraph 9)** (CORRECT, 68%)
- C. "Sylvie looked around. A balloon arch spelled 'Happy Make a Difference Day'..." (paragraph 19)
- D. "Sylvie nodded. She squeezed in a few warm-up stretches and reached for her megaphone." (paragraph 26)

### Example Informational MC Questions (2025, "Raccoons")

**Q20** (Key Details, NY-3.R.3): *Which sentence best explains why raccoons mostly come out at night?*
- A. Raccoons find their food when it is dark.
- B. Raccoons spend most of their time alone.
- **C. Raccoons hide during the day to keep safe.** (CORRECT, 36% -- hardest ELA MC)
- D. Raccoons have black masks and tail rings.

**Q22** (Cross-Text Connection, NY-3.R.9): *Animals learn to do things a certain way because of their habitat. Which sentence from the passage best connects to this idea?*
- A. "Raccoon paws have five fingers, just like a person's hands do." (paragraph 2)
- **B. "Exactly what a raccoon eats depends on where it lives." (paragraph 4)** (CORRECT, 41%)
- C. "They use the many nerves on the bottoms of their paws to feel their food." (paragraph 5)
- D. "In this home range, they have many hiding places that they use as dens." (paragraph 8)

---

## 4. Actual Released Constructed-Response Questions

### ELA Constructed-Response Examples (all worth 2 credits)

**2025 Q24** (Vocabulary): "What does 'pests' mean as it is used in paragraph 6? Use two details from the passage to support your response." (Avg: 1.06/2)

**2025 Q25** (Central Idea): "What is a central idea of the passage? Use two details from the passage to support your response." (Avg: 1.20/2)

**2025 Q32** (Character Analysis): "In 'The North Wind and the Sun,' what do lines 9 through 12 show about the North Wind? Use two details from the poem to support your response." (Avg: 1.04/2)

**2024 Q24** (Theme): "What theme is best supported by paragraphs 17 through 25 of 'Excerpt from The Little Red Lighthouse and the Great Gray Bridge'? Use two details from the story to support your response." (Avg: 0.96/2)

### Math Constructed-Response Examples

**1-Credit (answer only):**
- "What is the number 17,984 rounded to the nearest hundred?" Answer: 18,000
- "It takes Heidi 15 minutes to walk from her home to school. If she leaves her home at 8:35 a.m., what time will Heidi get to school?" Answer: 8:50 a.m.

**2-Credit (show work + explain):**
- "Cassandra wakes up at a quarter past 6 a.m. Her bus comes one-half hour later. At what time does Cassandra's bus come? Show your work."
- "Ms. Linsey and Mr. Abbott are comparing the sizes of their bulletin boards. Ms. Linsey's bulletin board is 6 feet long and 5 feet wide. Mr. Abbott's bulletin board is 7 feet long and 4 feet wide. Which bulletin board has the greater area? Be sure to include the area, in square feet, of each bulletin board in your answer. Explain how you found your answer."

**3-Credit (multi-part):**
- "Sam bakes cookies and puts all of the cookies into bags. If he puts 6 cookies into each of 6 bags, how many cookies does Sam bake? Show your work. [Part 2] Sam also bakes the same number of brownies as cookies. He puts all of the brownies into bags with 4 brownies in each bag. How many bags does Sam use for all of the brownies? Show your work."

---

## 5. Scoring Rubrics

### ELA 2-Credit Rubric
- **2 credits**: Valid inference from text + sufficient text-based evidence (2 details) + complete sentences
- **1 credit**: Mostly literal recounting, or only some relevant details, or incomplete sentences
- **0 credits**: Does not address the prompt, totally inaccurate, not in English, or indecipherable

### Math Scoring -- Critical Policies
- **Policy #3**: A correct answer with NO work shown on a 2- or 3-credit question receives **0 credits**
- **Policy #10**: Equations must be written **horizontally** (vertical format loses credit)
- **2 credits**: "Thorough understanding" -- correct answer + correct explanation
- **1 credit**: "Partial understanding" -- correct answer but incomplete explanation, or wrong answer with valid procedures
- **0 credits**: "Not sufficient" -- wrong answer with no valid work, or correct answer with zero work

---

## 6. Question Stem Wording Patterns

### ELA stems follow these recurring templates:

**Vocabulary**: "What does [word] mean as it is used in paragraph X?" / "Which meaning of the word [word] is used in paragraph X?"

**Central Idea**: "Which detail best expresses a central idea of the story?" / "Which detail would be most important to include in a summary?"

**Character**: "What do the details in paragraphs X and Y show about [character]?" / "By the end of the story, how does [character] most likely feel?"

**Text Structure**: "In which section would the reader find information about...?" / "Why is the information in paragraph X important to the story?"

**Author's Perspective**: "Which detail best shows how the author feels about [topic]?"

**Cross-text**: "[General principle statement]. Which paragraph/detail best shows how this idea is demonstrated?"

**All CR stems**: "[Analytical question]? Use two details from the passage/story/poem to support your response."

### Math stems follow these recurring templates:

**Expressions**: "Which expression represents how to find...?" / "Which expression is equivalent to...?"
**Fractions**: "Which fraction has a value equivalent to...?" / "Which two fractions are equivalent?"
**Area**: "What is the area, in square units, of the rectangle?"
**CR prompts**: "Show your work." / "Explain how you found your answer." / "Be sure to include what you know about [concept] in your answer."

---

## 7. Annotated Examples Showing Why Answers Are Correct

The 2014 annotated items provide full rationales. Key example from the "Sugaring Time" passage:

**Question**: "The first illustration best helps the reader understand the information in which paragraph?"

**Why B (paragraph 4) is correct**: The illustration shows sap flowing up toward branches in daytime and back to roots at night, which directly mirrors paragraph 4's description of daytime and nighttime sap movement.

**Why A (paragraph 2) is wrong**: While paragraph 2 mentions sap, it contains no information about the day/night cycle shown in the illustration.

**Why C (paragraph 5) is wrong**: Paragraph 5 mentions sap movement but does not address the day/night distinction shown in the illustration.

**Why D (paragraph 7) is wrong**: Paragraph 7 focuses on what syrup makers do with buckets, not the natural processes shown in the illustration.

---
